findings.description: Repeated page header/footer content in HTML
findings.evidence: The following text blocks repeat 3+ times, likely page headers/footers that should not appear in the body: 'where,' (3x).

findings.description: Company name and license number duplicated on the cover page in the HTML conversion
findings.evidence: In the PDF, 'Geosyntec Consultants of NC, P.C.' and 'NC License No.: C-3500 and C-295' appear once below the logo. In the HTML, these two lines appear twice — once in small text associated with the logo and again as separate regular-sized text — before the DocuSign Envelope ID and document title.

findings.description: Geosyntec page header/branding rendered as inline content between the TABLES section divider and Table 1, disrupting reading flow
findings.evidence: In the PDF, page 12 is a 'TABLES' divider page and page 13 shows Table 1 with its own page header. In the HTML, the Geosyntec logo, 'consultants', and 'Geosyntec Consultants of NC, P.C. / NC License No.: C-3500 and C-295' appear as inline content between the 'TABLES' heading and the 'TABLE 1 / PFAS ANALYTICAL METHODS AND ANALYTE LIST' title block, inserting repeated header material into the reading flow.

findings.description: Figure 3 flowchart image (PDF page 17) is missing from the HTML. The PDF shows a substantive flowchart diagram for 'Determining the Concentration for Loading at the River Water Intake,' but the HTML has no corresponding tag. Instead, the flowchart content is only rendered as a nested text list.
findings.evidence:
PDF page 17 contains a flowchart image with decision diamond ('Was a 24-hr composite sample collected?') and outcome boxes. HTML replaces this with: '
  • Was a 24-hr composite sample collected?
    • YES: Use 24-hr composite sample concentration*...' — no tag exists for Figure 3.

findings.description: In Equations 2 and 3, the PDF uses lowercase 'c' for individual PFAS compound concentration (c_OF002,j,i and c_intake,k,i) to distinguish it from uppercase 'C' for total PFAS concentration. The HTML renders both as uppercase 'C', losing this meaningful notational distinction.
findings.evidence: PDF Equation 2 second line: '∑ c_OF002,j,i × Q_OF002,j × F' (lowercase c). HTML: '= Σ (from i=1 to I) C<sub>OF002,j,i</sub> × Q<sub>OF002,j</sub> × F' (uppercase C). Similarly in the 'where' clauses: PDF uses 'c_OF002,j,i' but HTML shows 'C<sub>OF002,j,i</sub>'. Same issue repeats in Equation 3 with c_intake,k,i.
